assumptions: The dividend has already been declared and recognised as an intragroup receivable/payable, so it is analysed as a recognised item rather than a forecast transaction.
The analysis is for consolidated financial statements, not separate or individual financial statements.
No facts indicate that the group is applying the investment-entity exception mentioned in IFRS 9 paragraph 6.3.5.
It is not stated whether the entities have different functional currencies or whether the related foreign exchange gains or losses are not fully eliminated on consolidation.

approaches.label: General intragroup prohibition in consolidation
approaches.reasoning: In consolidated financial statements, IFRS 9 generally limits hedged items to exposures with parties external to the reporting entity. Intragroup items are therefore excluded unless a specific exception applies.
approaches.practical_implication: An intragroup dividend receivable is not hedge-designatable in consolidation by default.

source_contributions.summary: Paragraph 6.3.5 says only external-party assets, liabilities, firm commitments or highly probable forecast transactions can be hedged items in consolidated financial statements.

approaches.label: Intragroup monetary item exception
approaches.reasoning: IFRS 9 allows the foreign currency risk of an intragroup monetary item to be a hedged item in consolidated financial statements if the related FX gains or losses are not fully eliminated on consolidation.
conditions: The recognised dividend receivable/payable is a monetary receivable/payable.
The counterparties have different functional currencies.
The foreign currency exposure creates gains or losses that are not fully eliminated on consolidation.
The hedge relationship satisfies the general qualifying criteria in IFRS 9.
approaches.practical_implication: A recognised intragroup dividend receivable may be designated in consolidation, but only for the surviving FX exposure.

source_contributions.summary: Paragraph 6.3.6 permits foreign currency risk on intragroup monetary items to qualify in consolidated financial statements when FX gains or losses are not fully eliminated.

approaches.label: Foreign currency risk component designation
approaches.reasoning: IFRS 9 permits designation of a component of an item, including a specific risk, if it is separately identifiable and reliably measurable. This only works if the underlying intragroup receivable already qualifies as a hedged item.
conditions: The underlying dividend receivable qualifies under the intragroup monetary item exception.
The designated FX risk component is separately identifiable and reliably measurable.
The hedge documentation identifies the hedged item, hedged risk and hedge effectiveness assessment.
approaches.practical_implication: The hedge can be scoped to the FX component of the intragroup dividend receivable rather than the whole item.

source_contributions.summary: Paragraph 6.3.7 allows designation of a component of an item as the hedged item.
Paragraphs B6.3.8 and 6.4.1 require the risk component to be separately identifiable, reliably measurable, and formally documented within a qualifying hedge relationship.

approaches.label: Forecast intragroup transaction cash flow hedge
approaches.reasoning: IFRS 9 has a separate exception for highly probable forecast intragroup transactions affecting consolidated profit or loss. That approach does not fit once the dividend has already been recognised as a receivable.
approaches.practical_implication: Do not analyse an already recognised dividend receivable under the forecast-transaction exception.

source_contributions.summary: Paragraph 6.3.6 and paragraphs B6.3.5-B6.3.6 allow certain forecast intragroup FX exposures in consolidated cash flow hedges only when they are highly probable and affect consolidated profit or loss.

approaches.label: Net investment hedge alternative
approaches.reasoning: IFRIC 16 applies only to hedges of foreign currency risk arising from a net investment in a foreign operation and says it should not be applied by analogy to other hedge types. A dividend receivable is analysed as an intragroup monetary item, not the net investment itself.
approaches.practical_implication: The issue should be assessed under IFRS 9 intragroup-item rules, not under net investment hedge guidance.

source_contributions.summary: Paragraphs 7-8 limit the Interpretation to hedges of net investments in foreign operations and prohibit analogy to other hedge accounting types.
Paragraph 6.5.2 identifies net investment hedges as a separate hedge accounting category from fair value and cash flow hedges.
